heading: Limitation on interrogation techniques
label: 2000dd–2 Limitation on interrogation techniques
notes: References in TextExecutive Order 13491, referred to in subsec. (a)(6)(B)(i), is Ex. Ord. No. 13491, , 74 F.R. 4893, which is set out as a note under .Jan. 22, 2009section 2000dd of this title
CodificationSection was enacted as part of the National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2016, and not as part of the Detainee Treatment Act of 2005 which comprises this chapter.

children.heading: Limitation on interrogation techniques to those in the Army Field Manual
children.label: a Limitation on interrogation techniques to those in the Army Field Manual

children.heading: Army Field Manual 2–22.3 defined
children.label: 1 Army Field Manual 2–22.3 defined
children.text: In this subsection, the term “Army Field Manual 2–22.3” means the Army Field Manual 2–22.3 entitled “Human Intelligence Collector Operations” in effect on , or any similar successor Army Field Manual.November 25, 2015

children.heading: In general
children.label: A In general
children.text: An individual described in subparagraph (B) shall not be subjected to any interrogation technique or approach, or any treatment related to interrogation, that is not authorized by and listed in the Army Field Manual 2–22.3.

children.label: i
children.text: in the custody or under the effective control of an officer, employee, or other agent of the United States Government; or

children.label: ii
children.text: detained within a facility owned, operated, or controlled by a department or agency of the United States, in any armed conflict.

children.heading: Implementation
children.label: 3 Implementation
children.text: Interrogation techniques, approaches, and treatments described in Army Field Manual 2–22.3 shall be implemented strictly in accord with the principles, processes, conditions, and limitations prescribed by Army Field Manual 2–22.3.

children.heading: Agencies other than the Department of Defense
children.label: 4 Agencies other than the Department of Defense
children.text: If a process required by Army Field Manual 2–22.3, such as a requirement of approval by a specified Department of Defense official, is inapposite to a department or an agency other than the Department of Defense, the head of such department or agency shall ensure that a process that is substantially equivalent to the process prescribed by Army Field Manual 2–22.3 for the Department of Defense is utilized by all officers, employees, or other agents of such department or agency.

children.heading: Interrogation by Federal law enforcement
children.label: 5 Interrogation by Federal law enforcement
children.text: The limitations in this subsection shall not apply to officers, employees, or agents of the Federal Bureau of Investigation, the Department of Homeland Security, or other Federal law enforcement entities.

children.heading: Update of the Army Field Manual
children.label: 6 Update of the Army Field Manual

children.heading: In general
children.label: i In general
children.text: Not sooner than three years after , and once every three years thereafter, the Secretary of Defense, in consultation with the Attorney General, the Director of the Federal Bureau of Investigation, and the Director of National Intelligence, shall complete a thorough review of Army Field Manual 2–22.3, and revise Army Field Manual 2–22.3, as necessary to ensure that Army Field Manual 2–22.3 complies with the legal obligations of the United States and the practices for interrogation described therein do not involve the use or threat of force.November 25, 2015

children.heading: Availability to the public
children.label: ii Availability to the public
children.text: Army Field Manual 2–22.3 shall remain available to the public and any revisions to the Army Field Manual 2–22.3 adopted by the Secretary of Defense shall be made available to the public 30 days prior to the date the revisions take effect.

children.heading: Report on best practices of interrogations
children.label: B Report on best practices of interrogations

children.heading: Requirement for report
children.label: i Requirement for report
children.text: Not later than 120 days after , the interagency body established pursuant to Executive Order 13491 (commonly known as the High-Value Detainee Interrogation Group) shall submit to the Secretary of Defense, the Director of National Intelligence, the Attorney General, and other appropriate officials a report on best practices for interrogation that do not involve the use of force.November 25, 2015

children.heading: Recommendations
children.label: ii Recommendations
children.text: The report required by clause (i) may include recommendations for revisions to Army Field Manual 2–22.3 based on the body of research commissioned by the High-Value Detainee Interrogation Group.

children.heading: Availability to the public
children.label: iii Availability to the public
children.text: Not later than 30 days after the report required by clause (i) is submitted such report shall be made available to the public.

children.heading: International Committee of the Red Cross access to detainees
children.label: b International Committee of the Red Cross access to detainees

children.heading: Requirement
children.label: 1 Requirement
children.text: The head of any department or agency of the United States Government shall provide the International Committee of the Red Cross with notification of, and prompt access to, any individual detained in any armed conflict in the custody or under the effective control of an officer, employee, contractor, subcontractor, or other agent of the United States Government or detained within a facility owned, operated, or effectively controlled by a department, agency, contractor, or subcontractor of the United States Government, consistent with Department of Defense regulations and policies.

children.label: A
children.text: to create or otherwise imply the authority to detain; or

children.label: B
children.text: to limit or otherwise affect any other individual rights or state obligations which may arise under United States law or international agreements to which the United States is a party, including the Geneva Conventions, or to state all of the situations under which notification to and access for the International Committee of the Red Cross is required or allowed.
